text: Lipopeptides (LPs) are a broad class of amphiphilic compounds composed of a peptide headgroup covalently attached to one or more lipid tails. This review highlights the biophysical properties and biomedical applications of both naturally occurring and synthetic LPs. The first section addresses two main groups of bacterially derived cyclic LPs such as daptomycin and surfactin, which act as broad-spectrum antimicrobial and antifungal agents, and linear LPs like Toll-like receptor (TLR) agonists applied in immunotherapy, and bio-inspired sequences similar to that of the cosmeceutical ingredient Matrixyl. The self-assembly behavior of the LPs is analyzed in the second section in relation to their molecular structure and their response to changes in the solution environment such as pH and temperature. This is shown to affect their spontaneous self-assembly into nanostructures including, for example, liposomes, micelles (spherical and wormlike), nano-discs, and bilayer nanotapes and fibrils. In the third section, the complexity of the relationship between LP self-assembly and their function is highlighted. This includes a discussion of their increased biomedical efficacy resulting from their high density presentation on their nanostructured assemblies, and their possible interaction with cellular membranes.
